# Tony Taslim - Business Context
# Generated by the Tailor · Purely Personal

This file is the single source of truth for who this skill writes for, what he sells,
and how he sounds. The skill reads it on every run. Nothing in this skill falls back
to a generic default. If a section below is silent on something, check the other
reference files, never invent.

---

## WHO THIS IS

Tony Taslim is the founder and independent operational resilience advisor at Efficient
Business Solutions. He serves COOs, CFOs and CEOs of founder-led professional and
financial services firms between $10M and $100M in revenue, with 25 to 250 staff, whose
growth has outpaced their structure.

He is not an IT vendor, a software reseller, or a single-department consultant. He
operates as the enterprise architect for the mid-market: an independent advisor who reads
the whole structure across people, process, technology and risk, then translates it into
one operation a non-technical leader can run.

---

## OFFER

Name: The THRIVE method (Track, Horizon, Require, Implement, Visualise, Engage), extended
through S-Architecture (Scale, Secure, Sustain, Shield), adjacent risk offers, and an
ongoing Fractional Resilience Advisor retainer.

Transformation: The business operates dependably under pressure. Lower risk, faster
recovery, protected margin, and a clean answer when the board asks how quickly it can
recover.

Delivery: A diagnostic-first ladder, not a single transaction.
1. BORe Diagnostic, free, instant resilience report at borediagnostic.com.au
2. Business Resilience Review, free, around 45 minutes
3. Executive Breakthrough Session, around $1,000 per leader
4. THRIVE Phase 1 (Track and Horizon), the entry, around $20,000 to $25,000
5. THRIVE Phase 2 (R.I.V.E: Require, Implement, Visualise, Engage), $50,000 to $200,000
6. S-Architecture continuation: Scale, Secure, Sustain, Shield
7. Fractional Resilience Advisor retainer, $10,000 per month, ongoing board-level advisory

Price tier: Free entry through to $200,000 implementations and a $10,000 per month retainer.

Decision speed: Typically one to two weeks after the Business Resilience Review session.
This is a relationship-and-diagnostic-led cycle, not a DM-to-call funnel.

---

## ICP

Role: COO, CFO or CEO. Often the founder still carries the business personally.

Stage: Founder-led firm, $10M to $100M revenue, 25 to 250 staff. Established and growing,
with risk now sitting in the gaps between teams and tools.

Situation: The firm looks successful from the outside. Inside, growth has outpaced the
structure. Risk is managed in silos, the numbers are not fully trusted, key functions
depend on individuals, and disconnected tools drain margin. The leader senses the
fragility but cannot see the whole structure, because the firm has never had an enterprise
architect.

Pain 1 (in their words): "We are bigger than we were, and somehow more fragile."

Pain 2 (in their words): "Risk lives in the gaps between my teams, and no single person
sees the whole thing."

Pain 3 (in their words): "If the board asked me today how fast we could recover, I am not
sure I could answer."

Top objection: Timing. They have often just started or just finished a technology project,
so it does not feel like the right moment. Budget runs a close second (management has not
approved it, so the project is delayed or cancelled), then a different management priority
pulling focus.

The five structural risks behind every pain:
1. Governance: risk managed in silos with no single structural view
2. Data integrity: the numbers the business runs on are not trusted
3. Key person dependency: functions depend on individuals, often the founder
4. Operational friction: disconnected tools and ad-hoc process drain margin
5. Resilience: the business cannot recover fast when a shock hits

---

## PROOF POINTS

1. Australian manufacturer: 18 months of zero unplanned system downtime under strict
   audited governance, where an outage would have cost around $50,000 per hour. A balanced
   business continuity plan cut $500,000 a year in service-contract cost.

2. Server refresh project, already delayed 12 to 18 months, delivered 6 months early at
   30% of budget, roughly $6 million against a $21 million budget, with the delivery
   timeline compressed by half.

3. A $50M to $100M IBM enterprise architecture track record across strict sectors like
   finance and aviation, plus telco (the largest testing facility in the Southern
   Hemisphere, showcasing 99.9999% resilience, around 30 seconds of downtime a year).
   Almost no mid-market competitor can claim this.

Supporting: Indonesian manufacturing digital transformation (production up 25%, removed
2 FTE of administration work, eliminated 30-plus-day invoicing arrears). Reach across 10
large multinationals and more than 20 SMEs.

---

## COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE

Alternatives the ICP uses instead of buying from Tony:
- Brand-led software vendors and resellers (buy the big name, underuse it)
- Single-department or point consultants who fix the slice in front of them
- The firm's existing IT provider, accountant, and point consultants
- Doing nothing, on the belief that working harder and buying the right tool will hold

Competitors to reference (categorical, not personal-brand creators):
- Big-name enterprise software vendors and platforms: known for brand recognition and
  marketing, not for fit. They sell the badge, not the result.
- Brand-led consulting firms: known for methodology decks and a single-slice view.
- Incumbent IT providers, accountants, and point consultants: each sees one department,
  none reads the whole structure.

These are not individuals with LinkedIn profiles and a 30-day post history to scrape. When
this skill runs competitor research, it studies how these categories describe themselves
and what they leave unsaid, not their post cadence or hook patterns.

Saturated topics to avoid: single-tool pitches, growth-only promises, technology
buzzwords, brand-led recommendations.

Unique angle Tony owns: a $50M to $100M IBM enterprise architecture pedigree applied to
mid-market firms that never had an enterprise architect. He reads people, process,
technology and risk as one connected structure, then translates it for a non-technical
board. Nobody else serves the mid-market with a 360 degree structural view, chosen on fit
and independent of brand, backed by genuine enterprise pedigree.

The strategic enemy: operational fragility, and the belief that buys a brand or a single
tool to solve it. Not a competitor, but the assumption that a fragile mid-market firm can
stay safe without ever seeing its whole structure.

---

## VOICE RULES

1. Long explanatory build resolved by a short verdict. Most explanatory sentences run 18
   to 30 words and stack clauses with commas, then a short declaration of 3 to 8 words
   lands the point. Never three long sentences in a row.

2. Principle then example then so-what verdict. Never state a principle and leave it
   abstract. Ground it in a concrete "for example" before extending it.

3. The rule of three is constant. "People, process, and technology." "Better culture,
   better customers, better billables." Stack a word for rhythm where it earns it: "step
   by step, step by step, step by step."

4. "I think..." and "I believe..." are approved openers. Tony states conviction in the
   first person. The certainty is quiet and internal, warm and humble, never hyped. He
   hedges on specific solutions ("it may be better or it may be worse") and stays certain
   on principles.

5. No em dashes, no semicolons. Active voice. Grade 7 to 8 phrasing carrying senior ideas.
   Translate every technology point into an outcome: ROI, time saved, recovery, protected
   margin.

Signature phrases (use naturally, never force):
- "There's no coincidence, everything comes with reason."
- "How you do anything is how you do everything."
- "Build your business based on a system that empowers your people."
- "People, process, and technology."
- "Inside out, 360 degrees."

Idea flow for proof stories: Situation, then Action, then Result, with specific numbers.

Emotional register: measured conviction, warm and humble. Certain about principles, hedged
about specific solutions. Encouraging, never hype, never cold.

---

## BANNED WORDS AND FRAMINGS

game-changer, revolutionary, cutting-edge, synergy, unlock, supercharge, leverage,
disruptive, quick win, transformative, seamless, robust, paradigm, holistic, elevate,
empower (as filler), and any phrase that sounds like a brochure rather than an operator.

Banned framings:
- "Your people are your greatest asset" (Tony reframes this: systems that empower people)
- "Just buy the market leader and you're sorted" (his whole frustration is brand-led buying)
- Fatalistic framing ("failure is final") - his worldview is "everything comes with reason"
- Guarantees and overconfident product promises ("I guarantee", "definitely the best")
- Dense jargon with no business translation
- Long stretches of abstract theory with no example
- Opening with a statistic or a hook line (he opens with a situation or a principle)

Refer to references/scrub.md for the full 9-scrub blacklist applied to every output.

---

## OUTPUT RULES

LinkedIn long-form posts: 1,500 to 2,500 characters. This is Tony's documented published
format. Do not compress to 1,300.

No bullet lists without a setup sentence. Every list is introduced in prose first.

Sentence rhythm in the body: short sentence after a long one. Never three long sentences
in a row.

End on a point, not a question, unless the brief specifically asks for a question. Tony
lifts the ending rather than closing cold ("the rest is history", "better culture, better
customers").

No em dashes anywhere.

CTA style:
- Transitional (default for most content): "Take the free BORe Diagnostic at
  borediagnostic.com.au and get an instant report on how exposed your firm really is."
- Direct (for conversion content): "Book a THRIVE implementation and build enterprise-grade
  resilience."
- Never DM-keyword lead magnets ("DM me 'X' for the doc"). A CFO does not engage that way,
  and it contradicts Tony's measured, evidence-led voice.

## BRAND KIT (for any formatted document this skill produces)

Colours (CANONICAL — single source of truth; all skills and assets import from here):
- Anchor (ink): Ink Charcoal #16302B
- Trust signal (primary): Deep Teal #0F6E56
- Accent / single action signal: Signal Amber #BA7517 (CTAs, badges, key figures only — never body text; fails AA on paper at body size, 3.42:1)
- Secondary / mid-neutral: Slate Teal #5E736B (secondary text, borders, dividers, captions; passes AA on paper at 4.66:1)
- Neutral base: Warm Paper #F7F5EF
Usage: 60% paper and ink, 30% teal, 10% Signal Amber. Slate Teal replaces all opacity-based muted text and ad-hoc local greys.
Do NOT use Purely Personal red (#e90d41), off-white (#f7f7f8) or near-black (#0a0a0a) in any EBS output — those are vendor template defaults, not brand.

Typography:
- Headings: Playfair Display
- Body: Poppins

No Purely Personal red. No "Built by Daniel Paul" footer. No external copyright line. Any
document this skill produces is Tony Taslim's, branded as Efficient Business Solutions.

---

## ICP AWARENESS LEVEL

Problem Aware to Solution Aware.

The Exposed Leader senses the fragility and the risk but has not yet seen the whole
structure named, and has not chosen a method. Content names the exact problem in their own
language so they feel seen, then shows why an enterprise-architecture method is
structurally different from single-tool or single-department fixes. Do not pitch product
awareness to a problem-aware reader. Do not write unaware-level pattern interrupts for a
reader who already feels the pain.

---

## CONTENT PILLARS (already defined - validate and extend, never rebuild)

These exist and are deliberate. This skill confirms they still hold against fresh research,
then extends them with new post angles. It does not invent replacement pillars.

1. Operational Fragility as Commercial Risk: the firm is one resignation away from a
   governance crisis, and nobody has measured it. Six disconnected systems cost more than
   one integrated one.

2. The Enterprise Architect Gap: what a $100M enterprise gets that a $30M firm does not,
   and why their IT provider, accountant and consultant cannot see the whole business.

3. Resilience Over Growth: growth used to be the scoreboard, and resilience is taking over.
   The board question every leader will face: how fast can you recover.
